questions.scenario:
An external probe reports:

```text
$ nc -vz app.example.com 443
Connection to app.example.com (203.0.113.25) 443 port [tcp/https] succeeded!
```

The browser still displays a privacy warning. What has the probe actually proven?

options.text: The certificate is valid for `app.example.com` because every open port 443 listener has a trusted certificate
The application returned HTTP 200 because the TCP service name is shown as `https`
DNS produced an address and TCP port 443 accepted a connection, but certificate validation and HTTP behavior still require separate checks
Nginx successfully proxied to the upstream because TCP reachability includes backend health

questions.explanation: The connection test proves basic name resolution and TCP reachability to port `443`. TLS identity, certificate trust, and HTTP response semantics happen after that connection opens. An open listener does not prove the certificate, proxy, or app is healthy. [RFC 8446](https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc8446.html) defines the TLS handshake that follows TCP setup.

questions.scenario:
A platform team has migrated `api.example.com` onto a shared HTTPS listener at `198.51.100.10`, but a legacy hostname remains the default virtual host. A monitoring probe reports the legacy certificate while browsers receive the API certificate. The team reproduces the difference with these commands:

```text
$ openssl s_client -connect 198.51.100.10:443 </dev/null
subject=CN = legacy.example.com

$ openssl s_client -connect 198.51.100.10:443 -servername api.example.com </dev/null
subject=CN = api.example.com
```

Which explanation identifies the monitoring defect?

options.text: The first probe omitted SNI, so the listener returned its default certificate instead of selecting the virtual host for `api.example.com`
The second probe bypassed the listener and connected to a different address selected from DNS
The first probe resumed an old TLS session, so the listener ignored the currently configured certificate
The listener selected certificates from the HTTP Host header, which the first probe sent only after certificate validation

questions.explanation: SNI carries the requested hostname in the TLS ClientHello so a shared listener can select the matching certificate. Without SNI, the listener can return its default virtual-host certificate. `-servername` does not modify DNS, and certificate selection is deterministic rather than a forward-secrecy rotation. [RFC 8446](https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc8446.html) defines ClientHello extensions and TLS negotiation.

questions.scenario:
After certificate renewal, browsers on long-lived laptops succeed, but a new webhook worker fails:

```text
$ curl -v https://hooks.example.com/receive
* SSL certificate problem: unable to get local issuer certificate
```

The leaf certificate is current and includes `hooks.example.com`. A clean probe counts only one certificate in the server response. What should be fixed first?

options.text: The HTTP handler status because webhook clients validate the response body before TLS
The DNS TTL because recursive caches store intermediate certificates with A records
The webhook JSON schema because issuer validation is an application parsing error
The server certificate file so it presents the leaf plus required intermediate certificates as a full chain

questions.explanation: A clean client cannot build trust when the server omits required intermediates. Browsers with cached intermediates can hide this server-side mistake. HTTP handlers and payloads are reached only after TLS succeeds, while DNS does not carry the certificate chain. [Let's Encrypt certificate documentation](https://letsencrypt.org/docs/certificates-for-localhost/) explains certificate trust and chain concepts.

questions.scenario:
A checkout client sends:

```text
POST /api/orders HTTP/1.1
Host: app.example.com
Content-Type: text/plain;charset=UTF-8

{"sku":"book-123","quantity":1}
```

The API returns `415 Unsupported Media Type` and logs `expected application/json`. Which change addresses the evidence?

options.text: Change the request to GET because GET is the only method allowed to carry JSON metadata
Send the body with `Content-Type: application/json` so the server can select the expected parser
Renew the TLS certificate because status 415 is returned during certificate validation
Remove the Host header because virtual-host selection changes every JSON body into plain text

questions.explanation: The request reached HTTP and the API rejected the declared media type. Labeling the JSON body as `application/json` matches the parser contract. The status is not a TLS error, POST is appropriate for creating an order, and Host is needed for virtual-host routing. [RFC 9110](https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc9110.html) defines HTTP methods, fields, and response semantics.

questions.scenario:
A latency probe reports:

```text
dns=0.011s connect=0.043s tls=0.089s first_byte=4.812s total=4.836s
```

DNS, TCP, and TLS values are consistent with the healthy baseline, but users report slow API responses. Where should the investigation start?

options.text: The DNS zone, because every first-byte delay is caused by recursive resolution
The firewall handshake, because `time_starttransfer` measures SYN retransmissions only
The server, proxy, application, or dependency path because most delay occurs after connection and TLS setup but before the first response byte
The certificate issuer, because the total time proves the root CA responded slowly to each request

questions.explanation: The early phases are fast, while the first byte arrives several seconds later. That points to request handling, upstream work, database or dependency latency, or proxy waiting. Certificate authorities are not contacted for every normal request, and the timing separates DNS and connection setup from server response delay. [Everything curl](https://everything.curl.dev/usingcurl/verbose/writeout.html) documents timing variables and write-out diagnostics.

questions.scenario: A frontend at `https://app.example.com` calls `https://api.example.com/orders` with an Authorization header. Direct `curl` succeeds, but the browser blocks the request after an OPTIONS preflight. Which API response headers are relevant to the browser decision? Select all that apply.

options.text: `Server`, because the proxy brand determines which browser origins are trusted
`Access-Control-Allow-Origin: https://app.example.com`
`Content-Length`, because body size grants permission to use custom request headers
`Access-Control-Allow-Methods` containing the intended HTTP method
`Access-Control-Allow-Headers` containing `authorization` and any other requested non-simple headers

questions.explanation: Browser CORS checks compare the requesting origin, method, and requested headers with the API's allow response. Server branding and content length do not grant cross-origin permission. A non-browser `curl` request can work because CORS is enforced by browsers. [MDN CORS guidance](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/HTTP/Guides/CORS) documents preflight and allow headers.

questions.scenario:
Two public requests both complete TLS and reach Nginx:

```text
/api/health  -> HTTP/2 502
error.log    -> connect() failed (111: Connection refused) while connecting to upstream

/api/report  -> HTTP/2 504
error.log    -> upstream timed out while reading response header from upstream
```

Which incident summary is accurate?

options.text: Both failures are DNS errors because 5xx responses mean the hostname never resolved
The 502 points to an unusable upstream connection, while the 504 means Nginx waited for the upstream response and timed out
The 502 proves the client certificate expired, while the 504 proves SNI selected the wrong virtual host
Both failures should be fixed first by increasing proxy timeouts because refused connections recover with more waiting

questions.explanation: A refused upstream connection commonly produces 502 because Nginx could not obtain a usable response. A 504 with an upstream timeout means the connection path existed but the response did not arrive in time. DNS and TLS already worked, and raising timeouts does not fix a refused listener. [RFC 9110](https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc9110.html) defines gateway status semantics.

questions.scenario:
A deployment begins redirecting forever:

```text
$ curl -L -v https://app.example.com 2>&1
< HTTP/2 301
< location: https://app.example.com/dashboard
< HTTP/2 301
< location: https://app.example.com/dashboard
```

Nginx terminates TLS and proxies to the app over HTTP. What should be checked first?

options.text: Whether the public certificate includes the backend's loopback IP as a Subject Alternative Name
Whether the DNS resolver is alternating between A and MX records on each redirect
Whether Nginx forwards the original scheme and the app trusts that proxy context instead of repeatedly forcing HTTPS
Whether the client uses port 22 because redirect responses require an SSH control channel

questions.explanation: The repeated Location value proves an HTTP redirect loop. Behind TLS termination, an app that does not trust `X-Forwarded-Proto` can believe every proxied request arrived over HTTP and redirect it back to the same HTTPS URL. Certificate SANs, DNS record types, and SSH do not explain repeated valid HTTP 301 responses. [RFC 9110](https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc9110.html) defines redirects and HTTP field semantics.
